name: Garri / Eba (cassava swallow)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
pairingAdvice: Vegetable soup (efo, edikang ikong) and a protein.
frequency: A few times a week, small.
logicNote: Turns to sugar fast. Portion and soup pairing are everything.

name: Pounded Yam
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
pairingAdvice: Egusi or vegetable soup plus meat or fish.
logicNote: Smooth and dense; easy to overeat. Slow it with fibre and protein.

name: Amala (yam flour)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
frequency: A few times a week, small.
logicNote: Slightly friendlier than white swallows but still a starch; portion matters.

name: Amala (plantain flour)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
frequency: Better choice among swallows; still small.
logicNote: Often a smarter swap than cassava or yam swallow.

name: Fufu / Akpu (fermented cassava)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
logicNote: High starch; pair and shrink the portion.

name: Semovita / Semolina swallow
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
logicNote: Refined wheat; spikes quickly.

name: Wheat swallow
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
frequency: A better swallow choice; keep portion sensible.
logicNote: More fibre than semo or eba; a common recommended swap.

name: Oat swallow
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
logicNote: One of the friendliest swallows; more fibre, slower rise.

name: Tuwo Shinkafa (rice swallow)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
pairingAdvice: Miyan kuka or vegetable soup plus protein.
logicNote: Soft white rice paste; fast sugar.

name: Tuwo Masara (corn swallow)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
logicNote: Corn-based; a touch friendlier than rice tuwo.

name: Starch (cassava, Niger Delta)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
logicNote: Very dense starch, so keep to the size shown below.

name: White Rice (boiled)
portionGuidance: Half a cup of cooked rice. That is about the size of a tennis ball (90g).
frequency: Small, a few times a week.
logicNote: The classic spike. Cut portion, add fibre and protein to slow it.

name: Jollof Rice
portionGuidance: Half a cup of cooked rice. That is about the size of a tennis ball (90g).
pairingAdvice: Protein and a side salad or vegetables.
logicNote: Oil and tomato do not lower the sugar load; portion still rules.

name: Fried Rice
portionGuidance: Half a cup of cooked rice. That is about the size of a tennis ball (90g).
pairingAdvice: A palm-size piece of chicken or fish (90g), plus the mixed vegetables in it.
logicNote: Vegetables in it help slightly, but it is still white rice.

name: Ofada Rice (local unpolished)
portionGuidance: Three-quarters of a cup of cooked rice (about 120g).
frequency: A friendlier rice; still mind portion.
logicNote: Less polished than white rice, so a slower rise. A good swap.

name: Brown Rice
portionGuidance: Three-quarters of a cup of cooked rice (about 120g).
frequency: A better everyday rice; keep portion sensible.
logicNote: More fibre than white rice; the smarter default swap.

name: Basmati Rice
portionGuidance: Three-quarters of a cup of cooked rice (about 120g).
logicNote: Lower GI than ordinary white rice.

name: Rice and Beans
portionGuidance: Three-quarters of a cup, with more beans than rice (about 130g).
frequency: Decent option; beans slow the rice.
logicNote: The beans add fibre and protein, which softens the rice spike.

name: Boiled Yam
portionGuidance: Two small pieces. Each is about the size of a matchbox (about 100g).
pairingAdvice: Egg sauce, vegetable sauce, or fish stew.
logicNote: Dense starch; pair with protein and keep pieces few.

name: Fried Yam (dundu)
portionGuidance: Avoid. If eaten, two small pieces at most (about 80g).
pairingAdvice: If eaten, a protein and pepper sauce.
logicNote: Starch plus deep frying. Worst of both.

name: Yam Porridge (asaro)
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: Lots of vegetables in it, plus fish.
logicNote: Load it with vegetables and protein to slow the starch.

name: Roasted Yam (boli yam)
portionGuidance: Two small pieces. That is about the size of two matchboxes (100g).
pairingAdvice: Palm oil sauce with pepper and fish.
logicNote: Better than fried, still a dense starch.

name: Sweet Potato (boiled)
portionGuidance: One medium piece. That is about the size of your fist (120g).
pairingAdvice: Egg sauce or vegetables and protein.
frequency: A friendlier tuber; keep it boiled, not fried.
logicNote: More fibre than white yam; a reasonable swap when boiled.

name: Irish Potato (boiled)
portionGuidance: Two small potatoes. That is about the size of two eggs (120g).
logicNote: Higher GI than sweet potato; mind portion.

name: Cocoyam (boiled)
portionGuidance: Two small pieces. That is about the size of two eggs (120g).
logicNote: Lower GI than yam; a decent swap.

name: Boiled Plantain (unripe / green)
portionGuidance: About four to five slices, roughly one cup (120g).
logicNote: Unripe means less sugar and a slower rise. Friendly.

name: Boiled Plantain (ripe / yellow)
portionGuidance: About two to three slices (80g).
logicNote: The riper and sweeter, the faster the sugar.

name: Fried Plantain (dodo)
portionGuidance: Avoid. If eaten, three to four small slices at most (60g).
logicNote: Ripe sugar plus frying oil. Spikes hard.

name: Unripe Plantain Porridge
portionGuidance: One bowl. That is about one cup (200g), with plenty vegetables.
pairingAdvice: Plenty vegetables and fish in it.
logicNote: Unripe plantain plus vegetables is a friendly Nigerian meal.

name: Plantain Chips (fried)
portionGuidance: Avoid. If at all, a small handful. That is about 15 chips (30g).
logicNote: Fried, salty, easy to overeat.

name: Popcorn (plain)
portionGuidance: One cup, unsweetened (about 8g).
logicNote: Plain is OK in small amounts; caramel and sugar versions are red.

name: Abacha (African salad)
portionGuidance: A small plate. That is about one cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: Fish, ugba, garden egg, vegetables in it.
logicNote: Cassava base; the vegetables and fish help.

name: Suya
portionGuidance: One stick. That is about six to eight pieces (90g of meat).
logicNote: Spiced grilled meat; low carb. A great street-food choice.

name: Kilishi
portionGuidance: Three to four pieces (about 30g).
frequency: OK; some versions add sugar or groundnut paste.
logicNote: Mostly protein; check for sweet coatings.

name: Nkwobi
portionGuidance: One small bowl (about 150g).
logicNote: Cow foot in spicy sauce; low carb.

name: Boli (roasted plantain)
portionGuidance: Half of one medium unripe plantain.
pairingAdvice: Groundnut or pepper sauce and fish.
logicNote: Unripe boli with groundnut is the friendlier version.

name: Chin Chin
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, a small handful (30g).
logicNote: Fried sweet flour; spikes fast.

name: Puff Puff
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only. One ball the size of a golf ball (about 40g).
logicNote: Fried sweet dough; one of the worst snacks.

name: Meat Pie
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, half of one.
logicNote: Refined flour pastry; fast rise.

name: Sausage Roll / Gala
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one.
logicNote: Refined pastry around processed meat.

name: Buns
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only. One piece the size of a golf ball (about 50g).
logicNote: Fried sweet dough.

name: Doughnut
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, half of one.
logicNote: Fried, sugared dough.

name: Biscuits (sweet)
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, two small biscuits.
logicNote: Refined flour and sugar.

name: Cake
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one thin slice, a finger wide.
logicNote: Sugar and refined flour.

name: Ice Cream
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one small scoop.
logicNote: Concentrated sugar and dairy.

name: Cooked Beans (brown / honey)
portionGuidance: One cupped handful. That is about half a cup cooked (130g).
pairingAdvice: Fish, an egg, or two slices of plantain (80g).
logicNote: High fibre and protein, slow rise. One of the best diabetic-friendly staples.

name: Ewa Agoyin
portionGuidance: One cupped handful. That is about half a cup, easy on the oil (130g).
pairingAdvice: Add fish, meat, or egg. Skip the bread.
frequency: Good, but watch the oily sauce.
logicNote: Beans are great; the heavy palm-oil sauce is the only caution.

name: Moi Moi (bean pudding)
portionGuidance: One wrap. That is about the size of your palm (150g).
pairingAdvice: Stands alone or with a salad.
logicNote: Steamed beans, protein-rich, slow rise. A top pick.

name: Akara (bean cakes, fried)
portionGuidance: Three small balls (about 90g).
pairingAdvice: Pap is the problem, not the akara. Pair it with vegetables instead.
frequency: OK in moderation; mind the frying oil.
logicNote: Beans are friendly; deep frying is the only downside.

name: Beans Porridge
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: Fish, or two slices of unripe plantain (80g).
logicNote: Filling, high fibre, slow rise.

name: Okpa (Bambara nut pudding)
portionGuidance: One wrap. That is about the size of your palm (150g).
logicNote: Bambara nut, protein-rich, slow rise.

name: White Bread / Agege Bread
portionGuidance: One thin slice at most (30g), never on its own.
pairingAdvice: If eaten, egg and vegetables, never alone.
logicNote: Soft refined flour; one of the fastest spikes.

name: Whole Wheat Bread
portionGuidance: One to two slices (about 30g each).
logicNote: Check the label; many wheat breads are mostly white flour.

name: Pap / Akamu / Ogi
portionGuidance: One small cup made up (about 200ml), no sugar.
pairingAdvice: Moi moi or akara to slow it down.
frequency: Occasional; never with sugar or condensed milk.
logicNote: Smooth corn starch, fast rise. The sugar people add is the danger.

name: Custard
portionGuidance: One small cup made up (about 200ml), no sugar.
logicNote: Like pap; danger is the added sugar and milk.

name: Oats (plain)
portionGuidance: Half a cup dry (40g), made with water.
pairingAdvice: About 10 nuts. Skip sugar and sweet milk.
logicNote: Plain oats are friendly. Flavoured or instant ones often have added sugar.

name: Golden Morn
portionGuidance: Four tablespoons dry (about 40g), no sugar.
logicNote: Often eaten very sweet; that is the problem.

name: Cornflakes
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat, four tablespoons (30g).
logicNote: Refined corn plus usually sugar and sweet milk.

name: Boiled Corn
portionGuidance: Half of one medium cob.
logicNote: Pairing with pear or coconut slows the rise. Better than roasted.

name: Roasted Corn
portionGuidance: Half of one medium cob.
logicNote: Roasting raises the GI slightly versus boiled.

name: Egusi Soup
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size ball of swallow (100g) and plenty of vegetables.
logicNote: Melon seed and vegetables; friendly. The swallow beside it is what to watch.

name: Ogbono Soup
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size ball of swallow (100g) and plenty of vegetables.
logicNote: Low carb on its own; the swallow is the variable.

name: Efo Riro
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
logicNote: Vegetable-heavy and friendly.

name: Edikang Ikong
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
logicNote: Rich in vegetables and protein. One of the best.

name: Afang Soup
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
logicNote: Leafy and protein-rich; friendly.

name: Oha Soup
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size ball of swallow (100g) with fish, meat, or egg.
logicNote: Vegetable soup; watch only the thickener and swallow.

name: Bitterleaf Soup (Ofe Onugbu)
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size ball of swallow (100g) with fish, meat, or egg.
logicNote: Friendly vegetable soup.

name: Okra Soup (Okro)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
frequency: Excellent; okra itself helps blood sugar.
logicNote: Okra is one of the friendliest vegetables for diabetics.

name: Vegetable Soup (mixed)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
logicNote: More vegetables, better.

name: Banga Soup (palm fruit)
portionGuidance: One serving spoon, easy on the oil. That is about half a cup.
pairingAdvice: Fish, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
logicNote: Low carb; just rich in oil, so moderate the quantity.

name: Ewedu
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
pairingAdvice: Gbegiri and a fist-size ball of swallow (100g), with fish or meat.
logicNote: Pure leafy soup; very friendly.

name: Gbegiri (beans soup)
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: Ewedu and a fist-size ball of swallow (100g), with fish or meat.
logicNote: Bean-based, high fibre, slow rise.

name: Groundnut Soup (Miyan Gyada)
portionGuidance: One serving spoon. That is about half a cup.
pairingAdvice: Vegetables and fish or meat, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g).
logicNote: Groundnut adds protein and slows the meal.

name: White Soup (Ofe Nsala)
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: Fish or meat, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g).
logicNote: Light, low carb; watch only the yam thickener if used.

name: Pepper Soup
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one full bowl.
logicNote: Broth and protein, almost no carbs. A great choice.

name: Miyan Kuka (baobab leaf)
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g).
logicNote: Leaf-based, friendly.

name: Chicken (grilled / boiled)
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece. That is the size of a deck of cards (90g).
pairingAdvice: It goes with anything. Remove the skin for less fat.
logicNote: Grilled or boiled beats fried.

name: Beef (lean)
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece. That is the size of a deck of cards (90g).
logicNote: Trim visible fat.

name: Goat Meat
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece. That is the size of a deck of cards (90g).
logicNote: Lean red meat; friendly.

name: Fish (Titus / Mackerel / Tilapia / Catfish)
portionGuidance: One palm-size fillet. That is the size of a deck of cards (100g).
frequency: Excellent; one of the best proteins.
logicNote: Grilled, boiled, or peppered. Oily fish is especially good.

name: Eggs (boiled)
portionGuidance: One to two eggs.
pairingAdvice: Vegetables. It is great with two slices of unripe plantain (80g).
logicNote: No carbs, filling. Boiled beats fried.

name: Turkey
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece. That is the size of a deck of cards (90g).
logicNote: Lean if skin removed.

name: Snail
portionGuidance: Two to three medium snails (about 90g).
logicNote: Lean and friendly.

name: Prawns / Crayfish
portionGuidance: One handful of prawns (about 90g).
logicNote: Low carb seafood.

name: Pomo (cow skin)
portionGuidance: Two to three small pieces (about 60g).
frequency: OK; low nutrition but harmless for sugar.
logicNote: No carbs; fine for blood sugar.

name: Liver
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece (90g).
logicNote: Protein-rich; fine for blood sugar.

name: Fried Chicken / Fried Fish
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece (90g), not often.
logicNote: The protein is fine; deep frying is the only caution.

name: Ugu (pumpkin leaf)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one to two cupped handfuls.
logicNote: Leafy green; very friendly.

name: Waterleaf
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one to two cupped handfuls.
logicNote: Leafy green; friendly.

name: Spinach (efo tete)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one to two cupped handfuls.
logicNote: Friendly leafy green.

name: Garden Egg
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two to three whole garden eggs.
logicNote: Great low-sugar snack instead of sweet fruits.

name: Okra (vegetable)
portionGuidance: Eat freely. That is about one cup chopped.
logicNote: Often recommended for diabetics.

name: Cucumber
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one whole cucumber.
logicNote: Hydrating, almost no sugar.

name: Cabbage
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one to two cups shredded.
logicNote: Low carb, high fibre.

name: Carrot
portionGuidance: One to two medium carrots.
logicNote: Some natural sugar but low load; fine raw.

name: Tomato
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two to three whole tomatoes.
logicNote: Friendly base for stews.

name: Green Beans
portionGuidance: Eat freely. That is about one cup.
logicNote: Low carb, high fibre.

name: Ugba (oil bean)
portionGuidance: A small handful. That is about half a cup.
logicNote: Fermented oil bean; low sugar.

name: Avocado (Pear)
portionGuidance: Half of a medium avocado (75g).
logicNote: Healthy fat, almost no sugar. Great diabetic snack.

name: Pawpaw (Papaya)
portionGuidance: One slice. That is about half a cup diced (120g).
pairingAdvice: About 10 nuts to slow the sugar.
logicNote: Friendlier fruit, but still natural sugar; keep portion small.

name: Watermelon
portionGuidance: One cup diced. That is about one thin slice (150g).
pairingAdvice: About 10 nuts to slow the sugar.
logicNote: Mostly water but high GI; small portions only.

name: Orange
portionGuidance: One medium whole orange, not juice.
logicNote: Whole orange OK; orange juice is red.

name: Apple
portionGuidance: One small apple. That is the size of a tennis ball (120g).
logicNote: Lower GI fruit; the skin adds fibre.

name: Banana (ripe)
portionGuidance: Half of one medium banana.
logicNote: The riper the banana, the sweeter it is. Keep to the size shown below.

name: Mango
portionGuidance: Half of one small mango. That is about half a cup (80g).
logicNote: Sweet; small portions.

name: Pineapple
portionGuidance: One thin slice. That is about half a cup (80g).
logicNote: High GI; keep portions tiny.

name: Guava
portionGuidance: One small guava (about 55g).
logicNote: High fibre, lower GI; a friendly fruit.

name: African Star Apple (Agbalumo / Udara)
portionGuidance: One to two small fruits.
logicNote: Tart but sugary; small portions.

name: Soursop
portionGuidance: Half a cup. That is about three small pieces (100g).
logicNote: Lower sugar fruit.

name: Dates (dabino)
portionGuidance: One to two pieces only.
logicNote: Natural but very concentrated sugar.

name: Cashew Fruit
portionGuidance: One to two fruits.
logicNote: Juicy and sweet; small portions.

name: Groundnut (peanut)
portionGuidance: One small handful. That is a cupped palm (30g).
pairingAdvice: Great alone or with garden egg.
logicNote: Protein and fat, slow rise. A smart snack swap.

name: Cashew Nut
portionGuidance: One small handful. That is about 15 nuts (30g).
logicNote: Healthy fat; keep portion small for calories.

name: Tiger Nut (whole)
portionGuidance: One small handful (30g).
logicNote: High fibre; the whole nut is friendlier than the sweet drink.

name: Coconut
portionGuidance: Two to three small pieces (about 40g).
logicNote: Low sugar; pairs well to slow corn.

name: Walnut (Nigerian, asala / ukpa)
portionGuidance: One small handful. That is about seven whole (30g).
logicNote: Friendly low-sugar snack.

name: Soft Drink (Coke / Fanta / Sprite)
portionGuidance: None. Avoid completely.
logicNote: Liquid sugar; the fastest spike of all. The number one thing to drop.

name: Bottled Fruit Juice
portionGuidance: None. Eat the whole fruit instead.
logicNote: Even 100 percent juices are concentrated sugar without the fibre. Eat the whole fruit instead.

name: Malt Drink (Maltina / Amstel Malta)
portionGuidance: None. Avoid.
logicNote: Often thought healthy but very high sugar.

name: Energy Drinks
portionGuidance: None. Avoid completely.
logicNote: Sugar and caffeine; bad combination for blood sugar.

name: Zobo (no added sugar)
portionGuidance: One cup (250ml), no sugar.
logicNote: Hibiscus drink; friendly only if no sugar is added.

name: Zobo (sweetened)
portionGuidance: None. Avoid.
logicNote: The added sugar makes it as bad as a soft drink.

name: Kunu (sweetened)
portionGuidance: One small cup (200ml), unsweetened.
logicNote: Grain drink; the sugar version spikes. Unsweetened millet kunu is gentler.

name: Tiger Nut Drink (Kunu Aya)
portionGuidance: One small cup (200ml), no added sugar.
logicNote: Eat the whole tiger nut instead for more fibre.

name: Palm Wine
portionGuidance: One small glass (100ml), rarely.
logicNote: Alcohol plus natural sugar; both affect blood sugar.

name: Soaked Garri (drink)
portionGuidance: Avoid. If at all, one small cup (200ml) with no sugar.
pairingAdvice: If at all, with groundnut and no sugar.
logicNote: Cold soaked garri with sugar is a common hidden spike.

name: Milo / Bournvita / Ovaltine
portionGuidance: None of the sugary mix. Avoid.
logicNote: Chocolate malt drinks are mostly sugar.

name: Tea / Coffee (no sugar)
portionGuidance: One cup (250ml), no sugar.
logicNote: Friendly without sugar or sweetened milk.

name: Water
portionGuidance: Drink plenty, six to eight cups a day.
logicNote: The best drink for blood sugar, full stop.

name: Milk (full cream, e.g. Peak)
portionGuidance: Half a cup (125ml), unsweetened.
pairingAdvice: In tea or oats, no sugar.
logicNote: Has natural milk sugar; avoid sweetened condensed milk, which is red.

name: Condensed Milk (sweetened)
portionGuidance: None. Avoid.
logicNote: Loaded with added sugar.

name: Plain Yogurt (unsweetened)
portionGuidance: One small cup (150g), unsweetened.
logicNote: Unsweetened only; flavoured yogurts are full of sugar.

name: Sweetened / Flavoured Yogurt
portionGuidance: None. Avoid.
logicNote: Often as sugary as a soft drink.

name: Wara (local cheese)
portionGuidance: Three to four small pieces (about 90g).
logicNote: Soft cheese; low carb, protein-rich.

name: Palm Oil
portionGuidance: One to two teaspoons, use sparingly.
logicNote: No effect on blood sugar, but heavy for the heart; go easy.

name: Groundnut Oil / Vegetable Oil
portionGuidance: One to two teaspoons, use sparingly.
logicNote: No sugar impact; watch total quantity.

name: Olive Oil
portionGuidance: One to two teaspoons.
logicNote: Heart-friendly fat; no sugar impact.

name: Butter / Margarine
portionGuidance: One thin scrape. That is about one teaspoon.
logicNote: No sugar impact; the bread under it is the issue.

name: Mayonnaise / Salad Cream
portionGuidance: One teaspoon.
logicNote: Some sugar in salad cream; use lightly.

name: Sugar (table sugar)
portionGuidance: None. Avoid completely.
logicNote: The single thing to cut first.

name: Honey
portionGuidance: None. Avoid.
logicNote: Natural, but still pure sugar to your blood.

name: Glucose / Lucozade
portionGuidance: None, unless you are treating a low sugar. Then half a glass (100ml).
logicNote: Pure fast sugar. Only useful if blood sugar drops too low.

name: Salt
portionGuidance: Less than one teaspoon a day in total.
logicNote: No sugar impact, but too much raises blood pressure, which diabetics must watch.

name: Fura da Nono (millet and milk)
portionGuidance: One small cup (200ml), no added sugar.
logicNote: Millet balls in fermented milk. Gentle without sugar; the sweet type spikes.

name: Masa (rice cakes)
portionGuidance: Two small cakes (about 60g).
pairingAdvice: Miyan taushe or vegetable soup and protein.
logicNote: Fermented rice cakes, soft and starchy. Keep to the size shown below.

name: Dan Wake (bean dumplings)
portionGuidance: One small plate. That is about one cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: One teaspoon of oil and some pepper. Add plenty of vegetables.
logicNote: Bean-flour dumplings; high fibre and slow to raise sugar.

name: Miyan Taushe (pumpkin soup)
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size ball of swallow (100g) with fish, meat, or egg.
logicNote: Pumpkin and groundnut soup; filling and friendly.

name: Kuli Kuli (groundnut cake)
portionGuidance: A small handful (about 30g).
pairingAdvice: Eat it alone. If you add garri, keep the garri to a fist-size ball (100g).
logicNote: Fried groundnut cake; the nut is fine, the frying oil is the caution.

name: Dambu Nama (shredded meat)
portionGuidance: A palm-size amount (about 60g).
logicNote: Dried, spiced, shredded meat; mostly protein.

name: Ikokore (water yam porridge)
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: Plenty vegetables and fish in it.
logicNote: Grated water yam porridge; load it with vegetables and protein.

name: Ekuru (white moi moi)
portionGuidance: One to two wraps, palm size (150g).
pairingAdvice: Ata sauce and fish. Skip the eko.
logicNote: Steamed beans with no oil inside; protein-rich and slow.

name: Ojojo (water yam fritters)
portionGuidance: Three to four small fritters (about 80g).
pairingAdvice: Pepper sauce. Pair it with vegetables.
logicNote: Fried water yam balls; keep them few because of the frying.

name: Eko / Agidi (corn jelly)
portionGuidance: One small wrap (about 120g).
pairingAdvice: Beans, moi moi, or pepper soup.
logicNote: Set corn starch, smooth and quick to raise sugar. Keep to the size shown below.

name: Ukwa (African breadfruit)
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: One teaspoon of palm oil, plus fish.
logicNote: African breadfruit; high fibre and protein, a filling meal.

name: Fio Fio (pigeon pea pottage)
portionGuidance: One cupped handful. That is about half a cup (130g).
logicNote: Pigeon pea pottage; beans-like, high fibre and slow.

name: Achicha (dried cocoyam and beans)
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
logicNote: Dried cocoyam cooked with beans; the beans help slow it.

name: Ora Soup (Oha-style)
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size ball of swallow (100g) with fish, meat, or egg.
logicNote: Leafy vegetable soup; friendly, watch only the thickener and swallow.

name: Owho Soup (Urhobo)
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: Fish or bush meat, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
logicNote: Light palm-based Delta soup; low carb and friendly.

name: Fisherman / Native Soup
portionGuidance: One full bowl.
logicNote: Rich seafood broth, almost no carbs. A great choice.

name: Ekpang Nkukwo (grated cocoyam)
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: Plenty vegetables, periwinkle, and fish in it.
logicNote: Grated cocoyam wrapped and cooked; dense, so keep it moderate.

name: Periwinkle (isam)
portionGuidance: A small handful of the meat (about 60g).
pairingAdvice: In soups like afang and edikang ikong.
logicNote: Small shellfish; low carb and protein-rich.

name: Stockfish (okporoko)
portionGuidance: One small piece (about 60g).
logicNote: Dried fish; low carb and protein-rich.

name: Shaki (tripe)
portionGuidance: Two to three small pieces (about 90g).
logicNote: Cow tripe; low carb, fine for blood sugar.

name: Gizzard
portionGuidance: About five pieces, a palm-size serving (90g).
logicNote: Chicken gizzard; lean protein, low carb.

name: Crab
portionGuidance: One medium crab, or one handful of meat (90g).
logicNote: Shellfish; low carb and protein-rich.

name: Grasscutter (bush meat)
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece (90g).
logicNote: Lean bush meat; low carb.

name: Scent Leaf (nchanwu / efirin)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, a handful added to food.
pairingAdvice: In soups, pepper soup, and stews.
logicNote: Aromatic leaf; very low sugar and friendly.

name: Uziza (leaf and seed)
portionGuidance: A small handful of leaves, or a pinch of seed.
pairingAdvice: In soups like oha and nsala.
logicNote: Peppery leaf and seed; low sugar, adds flavour.

name: Utazi
portionGuidance: A small handful, sliced thin.
pairingAdvice: In abacha, nkwobi, and pepper soup.
logicNote: Slightly bitter leaf; low sugar and friendly.

name: Bell Pepper (tatashe)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one to two peppers.
logicNote: Sweet pepper; low sugar and high in fibre.

name: Onion (alubosa)
portionGuidance: One medium onion used in cooking.
logicNote: Low sugar and friendly; also good for the heart.

name: Lettuce
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two to three cups.
logicNote: Leafy and watery; almost no sugar.

name: Bitter Leaf (washed, as a side)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one to two cupped handfuls.
pairingAdvice: In soups and as a side.
logicNote: Leafy green; some studies link it to steadier blood sugar.

name: Ube (African pear)
portionGuidance: Two to three softened pears.
pairingAdvice: Great with boiled or roasted corn.
logicNote: Oily fruit; low sugar and high in good fat, and it slows corn.

name: Tangerine
portionGuidance: One medium fruit, not juice.
logicNote: Whole tangerine is fine; the juice is not.

name: Grapefruit
portionGuidance: Half of one medium fruit.
logicNote: Lower sugar citrus; a friendly fruit.

name: Lime and Lemon
portionGuidance: A squeeze in water or food.
logicNote: Very low sugar; adds flavour with almost no carbs.

name: Sugarcane (ireke)
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat, one small piece to chew.
logicNote: You chew out pure sugar; it raises blood sugar fast.

name: Velvet Tamarind (icheku / awin)
portionGuidance: A small handful of pods.
logicNote: Tart and sweet; small amounts only.

name: Tamarind (tsamiya)
portionGuidance: One to two pods, or a spoon of pulp.
pairingAdvice: In drinks and sauces, no added sugar.
logicNote: Sweet-sour pulp, and the sugar adds up. Keep to the size shown below.
carbExchange: one to two pods (a spoon of pulp)

name: Bitter Kola (orogbo)
portionGuidance: One to two seeds.
logicNote: Chewed seed; very low sugar.

name: Kola Nut (oji / gworo)
portionGuidance: One to two lobes.
logicNote: Low sugar, but the caffeine means do not overdo it.

name: Coconut Water
portionGuidance: One small glass (200ml), fresh and plain.
logicNote: Fresh coconut water is low in sugar; the packaged sweet type is not.

name: Jackfruit
portionGuidance: Three to four pieces. That is about half a cup (80g).
logicNote: Sweet tropical fruit; keep the portion small.
carbExchange: three to four pieces (half a cup)

name: Pomegranate
portionGuidance: Half a cup of seeds (about 80g).
logicNote: Has fibre but also natural sugar, so half a cup of seeds is fine.

name: Grapes
portionGuidance: About ten to fifteen grapes (80g).
pairingAdvice: About 10 nuts to slow the sugar.
logicNote: Small but sweet; easy to overeat, so count them.

name: Beef / Meat (regular cut)
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece. That is the size of a deck of cards (90g).
logicNote: Meat has no carbs, so it does not raise blood sugar. Trim the fat, as fatty cuts are heavy for the heart.

name: Fried Egg
portionGuidance: One to two eggs, fried in one teaspoon of oil.
pairingAdvice: One thin slice of bread (30g), a fist-size piece of yam (100g), or two slices of plantain (80g). Fry the egg in one teaspoon of oil.
logicNote: Egg has no carbs and does not raise your sugar. Fry it in one teaspoon of oil.

name: Scrambled Egg
portionGuidance: Two eggs, scrambled with pepper and onion.
pairingAdvice: Plenty of vegetables, with one thin slice of bread (30g) or a fist-size piece of yam (100g).
logicNote: No carbs from the egg. Add vegetables and keep the oil light.

name: Egg Sauce
portionGuidance: Two eggs cooked with tomato, pepper, and onion.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size piece of boiled yam (100g), two slices of plantain (80g), or one small boiled potato.
logicNote: The egg and vegetables are friendly. Watch the size of any yam or plantain beside it.

name: Omelette
portionGuidance: Two eggs, with vegetables mixed in.
pairingAdvice: One thin slice of bread (30g), or a fist-size piece of yam (100g).
logicNote: Egg plus vegetables is a friendly, filling meal. Keep the oil light.

name: Egg Roll
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one.
logicNote: A boiled egg wrapped in sweet fried dough. The dough is the problem, not the egg.

name: Scotch Egg
portionGuidance: One, as an occasional snack.
pairingAdvice: Alone, with a vegetable on the side.
logicNote: A boiled egg in minced meat, then fried. Better than egg roll, but the frying makes it a sometimes food.

name: Lafun (white cassava swallow)
portionGuidance: One ball the size of your fist. That is about half a cup (100g).
pairingAdvice: Vegetable soup like ewedu or efo, plus a protein.
frequency: A few times a week, small.
logicNote: Cassava swallow; turns to sugar fast, so portion and soup pairing matter most.

name: Coconut Rice
portionGuidance: Half a cup of cooked rice. That is about the size of a tennis ball (90g).
logicNote: Still white rice; the coconut does not lower the sugar. Cut the portion and add fibre.

name: Native Rice (palm oil rice)
portionGuidance: Half a cup of cooked rice. That is about the size of a tennis ball (90g).
pairingAdvice: It has vegetables and fish in it. Keep the rice to half a cup (about 130g).
logicNote: A one-pot rice with vegetables and fish, which helps, but a big rice portion still spikes.

name: Water Yam (boiled)
portionGuidance: Two small pieces. That is about the size of two matchboxes (100g).
pairingAdvice: Vegetable sauce, egg, or fish stew.
logicNote: Dense starch like white yam; keep the pieces few and pair with protein.

name: Gizdodo (gizzard and plantain)
portionGuidance: A small plate, about one cup (150g). Plantain with the gizzard.
pairingAdvice: The gizzard is the friendly part. Keep the fried plantain to about four slices.
logicNote: Gizzard is low-carb protein; the fried ripe plantain is the sugar to watch.

name: Adalu (beans and corn)
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: Fish, or two slices of plantain (80g). Keep the corn to about two tablespoons.
frequency: A few times a week, small.
logicNote: Beans are friendly; the corn adds some sugar, so keep the corn share small.

name: Beans and Plantain
portionGuidance: One cupped handful of beans with a few slices of plantain.
pairingAdvice: Use boiled or roasted plantain, not fried. Add fish.
logicNote: The beans are great; the plantain is the sugar. Boiled beats fried, and keep the plantain small.

name: Ofe Owerri
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: A fist-size ball of swallow (100g) with plenty of fish or meat.
logicNote: A rich Igbo vegetable and meat soup; low carb, watch only the swallow beside it.

name: Okazi Soup
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g) if any.
logicNote: Leafy Igbo soup, close to afang; friendly and low carb.

name: Editan Soup
portionGuidance: One to two serving spoons. That is about one cup.
pairingAdvice: Fish, meat, or egg, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g).
logicNote: Efik bitter-leaf style soup, rich in vegetables; friendly.

name: Atama Soup (Abak)
portionGuidance: One serving spoon, easy on the oil. That is about half a cup.
pairingAdvice: Fish and seafood, with a fist-size ball of swallow (100g).
logicNote: Palm-fruit and atama-leaf soup; low carb but rich in oil, so go easy on the oil.

name: Ofe Akwu (palm fruit sauce)
portionGuidance: One serving spoon, easy on the oil. That is about half a cup.
pairingAdvice: Fish or meat. Eat it with half a cup of white rice (about 130g).
logicNote: Palm-fruit sauce, low in carbs but rich in oil; the rice beside it is what to keep small.

name: Miyan Kubewa (okra soup, northern)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, two or more serving spoons.
logicNote: Northern okra soup; okra is one of the friendliest foods for blood sugar.

name: Asun (spicy goat meat)
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece. That is the size of a deck of cards (90g).
pairingAdvice: Onions and pepper, with a vegetable side.
logicNote: Grilled, peppered goat meat; low carb and friendly. Trim heavy fat.

name: Isi Ewu (goat head)
portionGuidance: One small bowl. That is about three-quarters of a cup (150g).
pairingAdvice: Utazi and onions. Eat it on its own.
logicNote: Spiced goat head; low carb. It is rich, so keep the portion modest.

name: Ram Meat (mutton)
portionGuidance: One palm-size piece. That is the size of a deck of cards (90g).
logicNote: Red meat with no carbs; trim visible fat, as fatty cuts are heavy for the heart.

name: Kidney (offal)
portionGuidance: One palm-size serving (90g).
logicNote: Organ meat; low carb and protein-rich. Fine in moderation.

name: Sardine (canned)
portionGuidance: Half a small tin.
pairingAdvice: With vegetables. Skip the bread if you can.
logicNote: Oily fish, low in carbs and rich in good fat. Choose the ones packed in water rather than oil.

name: Corned Beef (canned)
portionGuidance: A few tablespoons, about a quarter of a tin.
logicNote: Low in carbs, but processed and salty. Keep to the size shown below and drink water.

name: Soko (Celosia greens)
portionGuidance: Eat freely, one to two cupped handfuls.
logicNote: Leafy green; very low sugar and friendly.

name: Broccoli
portionGuidance: Eat freely, about one cup.
logicNote: Low carb, high fibre; a very friendly vegetable.

name: Cauliflower
portionGuidance: Eat freely, about one cup.
pairingAdvice: Stir-fry, or use in place of rice.
logicNote: Low carb and filling; some people use it in place of rice.

name: Pumpkin (flesh)
portionGuidance: About one cup, cooked.
pairingAdvice: In soups and porridge with protein.
logicNote: More fibre than yam; a friendlier soft food when cooked plain.

name: Strawberry
portionGuidance: About seven to eight berries (100g).
pairingAdvice: About 10 nuts, or one small cup of plain yogurt (150g).
logicNote: Lower sugar and high in fibre; a friendly fruit.

name: Golden Melon
portionGuidance: One cup diced (about 150g).
pairingAdvice: About 10 nuts to slow the sugar.
logicNote: Mostly water but still sweet; keep the portion to a cup.

name: Monkey Kola (achicha)
portionGuidance: Two to three small fruits.
logicNote: A local low-sugar fruit; friendly in small amounts.

name: Passion Fruit
portionGuidance: One to two fruits.
logicNote: Tangy with some natural sugar; small amounts only.

name: Almond Nuts
portionGuidance: One small handful. That is a cupped palm (30g).
logicNote: Healthy fat and fibre; slow to raise sugar. A smart snack.

name: Egusi (melon seed)
portionGuidance: In soup, or a small handful roasted (30g).
pairingAdvice: In soups, or roasted as a snack.
logicNote: Melon seed is protein and fat; low sugar. Mostly eaten in soup.

name: Chapman
portionGuidance: Avoid. It is a sweet cocktail.
logicNote: A mix of soft drinks and syrup; liquid sugar. Ask for a sugar-free version or skip it.

name: La Casera (apple drink)
portionGuidance: None. Avoid.
logicNote: A sweet bottled soft drink; liquid sugar like any soda.

name: Beer / Stout
portionGuidance: One small bottle at most, rarely.
pairingAdvice: Never on an empty stomach. Eat first.
logicNote: Alcohol can drop or raise your sugar, and beer carries carbs. If you drink, keep to the size shown below and eat first.

name: Pito / Burukutu (local brew)
portionGuidance: One small cup (200ml), rarely.
logicNote: Fermented grain brew with alcohol and some sugar; both affect blood sugar.

name: Ogogoro (local gin)
portionGuidance: Best avoided.
logicNote: Strong local spirit; alcohol can make your sugar swing and is hard on the body. Best to skip.

name: Fruit Smoothie
portionGuidance: One small cup (200ml), made from whole fruit with no added sugar.
pairingAdvice: One small glass (200ml) with a meal, not on its own. Better to eat the whole fruit.
logicNote: Even fruit smoothies are quick sugar. Make it small, use whole fruit, and never add sugar.

name: Nono (fermented milk)
portionGuidance: One small cup (200ml), no sugar.
logicNote: Plain fermented milk is friendly; the sweetened kind is not.

name: Evaporated Milk (unsweetened)
portionGuidance: One tablespoon in tea or pap, with no sugar.
pairingAdvice: In tea, oats, or pap, with no sugar.
logicNote: Unsweetened evaporated milk has natural milk sugar, so use one tablespoon. Avoid the sweetened condensed kind.

name: Weetabix / Wheat Cereal
portionGuidance: Two biscuits, with unsweetened milk.
logicNote: Wholewheat cereal is better than sugary ones, but still a starch; no added sugar.

name: Coconut Bread
portionGuidance: One thin slice at most (30g).
logicNote: Softer, sweeter bread; refined flour spikes fast. One thin slice, with protein.

name: Shawarma
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only. Half of one at most.
pairingAdvice: If you eat it, ask for more meat and vegetables and less bread and sauce.
logicNote: The wrap is refined flour and the sauces add sugar and fat. A once-in-a-while treat.

name: Samosa
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one or two small ones.
logicNote: Fried pastry; spikes fast. Keep to a rare treat.

name: Spring Roll
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one or two.
logicNote: Fried wrap around vegetables; the wrapper and the frying are the problem.

name: Fish Roll
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one.
logicNote: Fish in fried dough; the dough is refined flour that spikes fast.

name: Small Chops
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only. About four pieces at most.
logicNote: A mix of fried snacks like puff puff, samosa, and spring roll; all quick sugar and oil.

name: Donkwa / Dankwa (groundnut snack)
portionGuidance: A few small pieces (about 30g).
logicNote: Groundnut and grain snack. The nut is fine, but it has some sugar and grain, so keep to the size shown below.

name: Kokoro (maize snack)
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, a few.
logicNote: Fried maize sticks; refined and oily, spikes fast.

name: Aadun (corn snack)
portionGuidance: One small piece (about 40g).
logicNote: Cornmeal snack with oil and pepper. Still corn starch, so keep to the size shown below.

name: Robo (melon seed cake)
portionGuidance: A few pieces (about 40g).
logicNote: Fried melon-seed cake; the seed is friendly, the frying is the only caution.

name: Coconut Candy
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only, one small piece.
logicNote: Coconut cooked in sugar; the sugar makes it a red.

name: Cassava Chips
portionGuidance: Avoid. A rare treat only. A small handful, about 15 chips (30g).
logicNote: Fried cassava; starchy and oily, spikes fast.

name: Ginger
portionGuidance: Use freely in cooking and tea.
logicNote: Adds flavour with almost no sugar, and may be gentle on blood sugar.

name: Garlic
portionGuidance: Use freely in cooking.
logicNote: Low sugar and good for the heart; use as much as you like in cooking.

name: Locust Bean (iru / dawadawa)
portionGuidance: A spoon or two in soups.
logicNote: Fermented seasoning; low sugar and protein-rich. Good for flavour.

name: Seasoning Cube (Maggi / Knorr)
portionGuidance: One cube per pot, not more.
pairingAdvice: In cooking. Balance it with less added salt.
logicNote: No sugar impact, but very salty. Too much salt raises blood pressure, which diabetics must watch.

name: Ketchup
portionGuidance: One tablespoon at most.
logicNote: It has hidden sugar, so use one tablespoon, not a pool.

name: Pepper (fresh chili)
portionGuidance: Use freely for taste.
pairingAdvice: In stews, sauces, and pepper soup.
logicNote: Fresh pepper adds flavour with almost no sugar.

name: Coconut Oil
portionGuidance: One to two teaspoons, use sparingly.
logicNote: No sugar impact, but still a heavy fat, so use one teaspoon.

name: Sugar-free Sweetener
portionGuidance: A pinch, or one tablet, in place of sugar in tea or coffee.
logicNote: A stand-in for sugar with little or no effect on blood sugar. A better choice than sugar.
